coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance criteria gaps and verification completeness will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria gaps and verification completeness

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` greps the unanchored word '_processors_keys' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review__processors_keys'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"_processors_keys":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3's proving command to also directly import all 6 symbols from `_processors_identity.py` and (optionally) assert their `__module__` equals `rebar.reducer._processors_identity`, so both halves of the criterion ('live in leaf' AND 're-exported') are mechanically verified.
evidence: AC3 text: 'The 6 named symbols live in the leaf and are re-exported (# noqa: F401) from _processors.py so back-compat holds.'

Proving command: `python -c "from rebar.reducer._processors import process_key_event, process_signature, attestation_kind, _fold_author_attribution, _rederive_keyring_keys, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring; print('ok')`
ABSENCE: no command verifies `from rebar.reducer._processors_identity import <symbols>` succeeds, nor that the symbols' `__module__` attribute points to _processors_identity.
findings.finding: AC3's proving command only verifies that the 6 symbols are importable FROM _processors.py (re-export), but does NOT verify that they actually RESIDE in _processors_identity.py (the 'live in the leaf' half of the criterion). If a developer forgets to move the symbols and leaves them defined natively in _processors.py, the proving command still passes green — the riskiest part of the refactor (the actual move) goes unchecked.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (`python -c "from rebar.reducer._processors import process_key_event ..."')`
scenarios: Developer leaves all 6 functions defined in _processors.py (no actual move), adds a trivially empty _processors_identity.py of 100+ lines of comments, AC3 passes, LOC ACs pass, but the refactor goal (extract to leaf) is unachieved.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the single python -c line with two checks:
```bash
# Verify symbols reside in the leaf
python -c "from rebar.reducer._processors_identity import process_key_event, process_signature, attestation_kind, _fold_author_attribution, _rederive_keyring_keys, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring; print('leaf ok')"
# Verify back-compat re-export from _processors still resolves
python -c "from rebar.reducer._processors import process_key_event, process_signature, attestation_kind, _fold_author_attribution, _rederive_keyring_keys, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring; import rebar.reducer._processors_identity as m; assert process_key_event.__module__ == m.__name__, f'expected {m.__name__!r}, got {process_key_event.__module__!r}'; print('re-export ok')"
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the ceilings file format and update the AC #4 grep pattern to correctly match it (e.g., use `grep -q 'reducer/_processors\.py'` without a trailing space, or document the format assumption).
evidence: AC #4 allowlist pattern: `'_processors.py'` (no path, no trailing space)
AC #4 ceilings pattern: `'reducer/_processors.py '` (path prefix + trailing space) — the trailing space is load-bearing and format-dependent
The plan does not document the ceilings file format, so the trailing-space assumption is unverifiable from plan text alone
findings.finding: AC #4's verify command uses the pattern `'reducer/_processors.py '` (with a trailing space) for the ceilings file check, but uses `'_processors.py'` (no path prefix, no trailing space) for the allowlist check. If the ceilings file format does not include a trailing space after the path (e.g., the line is `reducer/_processors.py` or tab-delimited), the grep will never match and the `!`-negated check will silently pass even when the stale entry is still present — giving a false green on the removal criterion.
scenarios: Ceilings file uses `reducer/_processors.py\t867` or `reducer/_processors.py\n` format → grep misses it → AC #4 passes despite stale entry remaining → module-size contract test (AC #7) may still catch it, but AC #4's own verify is unreliable.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the ceilings grep to `! grep -q 'reducer/_processors\.py' .github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (no trailing space, escaped dot) so it matches the entry regardless of what follows the path on the line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and reconcile `_fold_author_attribution`'s call sites: it is called by `process_create`, `process_comment`, and `process_revert` (all staying in `_processors.py`) as well as `process_signature`; the plan must either (a) also move those three callers to the leaf, (b) keep `_fold_author_attribution` in `_processors.py` and have the leaf import it (documenting the one-way dep as acceptable), or (c) duplicate/inline it — the current spec creates an unavoidable import cycle.
evidence: Plan text: '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms'
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 150: _fold_author_attribution(state, event) — called by process_create
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 394: _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event) — called by process_comment
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 520: _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event) — called by process_revert
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 658: _fold_author_attribution(record, event) — called by process_signature
process_create, process_comment, process_revert are NOT in the 6 named symbols to move, so they remain in _processors.py needing to import _fold_author_attribution from the new leaf
findings.finding: The plan asserts '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller)' — implying process_signature is _fold_author_attribution's sole caller — but the code shows _fold_author_attribution is also called by process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520), all of which stay in _processors.py. Moving _fold_author_attribution to the leaf forces those three retained callers to import it back from _processors_identity.py, creating a _processors.py → _processors_identity.py dependency that the plan explicitly claims to avoid ('else a back-edge cycle forms').
findings.location: Plan body: 'DEVIATION from docs/architecture.md…' paragraph; _fold_author_attribution move rationale
scenarios: If _fold_author_attribution moves to the leaf, process_create/comment/revert in _processors.py must `from ._processors_identity import _fold_author_attribution`, creating the very back-edge the plan says to avoid.
If _fold_author_attribution stays in _processors.py instead (not moved), process_signature in the leaf would need to import it back from _processors.py, also creating a cycle.
The plan's no-cycle premise — that the 6 symbols 'reference only each other (a true leaf)' — is falsified by _fold_author_attribution's 3 additional callers that are not being moved.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep `_fold_author_attribution` in `_processors.py` (its primary use site is the 3 non-moved processors) and have `_processors_identity.py` do `from ._processors import _fold_author_attribution` — but only if that direction is acceptable. Alternatively, move it to a shared `_processors_common.py` that both modules import from. Update the plan to remove the false 'true leaf' claim for `_fold_author_attribution` and document the actual dependency direction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit `_bootstrap_genesis_keyring`'s call sites: it is invoked by `process_snapshot` (line 852 of `_processors.py`), which is not being moved; the plan must account for this cross-module call (either move `process_snapshot` to the leaf, keep `_bootstrap_genesis_keyring` in `_processors.py`, or document the import direction explicitly).
evidence: Plan text: 'These reference only each other (a true leaf): process_key_event calls _rederive_keyring_keys; process_signature calls attestation_kind and _fold_author_attribution'
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 852: _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, str(state.get('created_at') or '')) — called by process_snapshot
process_snapshot is not in the 6 named symbols to move
The plan's call-graph description omits process_snapshot → _bootstrap_genesis_keyring entirely
findings.finding: The plan asserts '_bootstrap_genesis_keyring' is part of a 'true leaf' cluster that only references the other 5 named symbols. But _bootstrap_genesis_keyring is also called by process_snapshot (line 852 of _processors.py), which is NOT in the move list. Moving _bootstrap_genesis_keyring to the leaf means process_snapshot must import it back from _processors_identity.py, again creating the back-edge the plan claims does not exist.
findings.location: Plan body: 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' assertion
scenarios: After the move, process_snapshot in _processors.py needs _bootstrap_genesis_keyring from the leaf, requiring `from ._processors_identity import _bootstrap_genesis_keyring` — a back-edge from _processors.py into the supposedly pure leaf.
If the implementer notices this at implementation time, they must choose to also move process_snapshot (large function with many dependencies) or restructure — neither of which is specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that _processors.py must also import _fold_author_attribution, attestation_kind, and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring FROM _processors_identity.py for its own internal callers (process_create, process_comment, process_revert, process_snapshot), not only re-export them with # noqa: F401; update the 'true leaf / reference only each other' rationale to reflect these inward callers.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:150: _fold_author_attribution(state, event)  [inside process_create, staying in _processors.py]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:394: _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event)  [inside process_comment, staying in _processors.py]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:520: _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event)  [inside process_revert, staying in _processors.py]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:834: kind = attestation_kind(sig.get('manifest'), {})  [inside process_snapshot, staying in _processors.py]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:213: _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, ...)  [inside process_create, staying in _processors.py]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:852: _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, ...)  [inside process_snapshot, staying in _processors.py]
ABSENCE: plan never describes 'from ._processors_identity import ...' in _processors.py for its own internal callers; only describes re-exports (# noqa: F401) for external consumers
findings.finding: The plan incorrectly describes the 6 extracted symbols as a 'true leaf' that reference 'only each other.' Three of the moved symbols are also called by functions staying in _processors.py: _fold_author_attribution is called by process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520); attestation_kind is called by process_snapshot (line 834); _bootstrap_genesis_keyring is called by process_create (line 213) and process_snapshot (line 852). Moving these symbols therefore requires _processors.py to import them FROM _processors_identity.py for its own internal use — a '_processors.py → _processors_identity.py' inward import the plan never describes. Without this import, process_create, process_comment, process_revert, and process_snapshot break at runtime.
findings.location: Plan body — 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' and '_processors.py imports them back and re-exports them (# noqa: F401)'
scenarios: Implementer adds only the # noqa: F401 re-exports to _processors.py (as the plan describes) but forgets to also add the functional imports needed by process_create, process_comment, process_revert, and process_snapshot — those functions break with NameError at runtime.
The 'true leaf' framing causes a reviewer to accept an implementation that doesn't import the moved symbols back for _processors.py's own use, shipping a broken reducer.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the plan body to say: '_processors.py imports all 6 symbols from the new leaf — both for re-export (# noqa: F401, so external consumers keep resolving) AND for its own internal use by process_create, process_comment, process_revert, and process_snapshot, which call _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind.' Drop or qualify the 'true leaf / reference only each other' language, since the leaf is a leaf with respect to the rest of the package (nothing else it imports cycles back through it), but the 6 symbols are not exclusively called by each other.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC5 and the docs/architecture.md file_impact entry to require deletion of the ENTIRE `| reducer/_processors.py | … |` offenders-table row (not just the _processors_keys sub-reference), and strengthen the AC5 verify command to assert the full row is gone (e.g., `! grep -q 'reducer/_processors.py' docs/architecture.md`).
evidence: Parent Phase 1: 'split on a logical seam, graduate the file off BOTH allowlist + ceilings + its architecture.md remedy row, land on main'

Leaf file_impact for docs/architecture.md: 'delete the stale sub-floor _processors_keys.py offenders-table remedy row' — describes only the sub-reference, not the full | reducer/_processors.py | … | table row
Leaf AC5: `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` — verifies only that the string '_processors_keys' is absent, not that the entire `reducer/_processors.py` offenders row is gone
docs/architecture.md line 276 contains the full offenders-table row for reducer/_processors.py that must be deleted when the file graduates per the parent contract
findings.finding: The parent epic mandates 'graduate the file off BOTH allowlist + ceilings + its architecture.md remedy row' for each Phase 1 story. The full remedy row for reducer/_processors.py is the entire offenders-table row (`| reducer/_processors.py | ... |`) in docs/architecture.md. The leaf's file_impact entry describes only 'delete the stale sub-floor _processors_keys.py offenders-table remedy row' (a sub-reference within the row), and AC5's verify command (`! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md`) passes even if the full `reducer/_processors.py` offenders row is merely edited to remove the '_processors_keys' mention rather than deleted entirely — leaving the graduated file's remedy row in the table, contrary to the parent's requirement.
findings.location: Leaf AC5 verify command and file_impact entry for docs/architecture.md; parent Phase 1 description
scenarios: Implementer removes only the '_processors_keys.py' text from the architecture.md row (leaving the rest of the | reducer/_processors.py | row intact) — AC5 passes, but the offenders-table row for a now-graduated file persists, contradicting the parent's graduation mandate.
A future reader of architecture.md sees the reducer/_processors.py offenders row and incorrectly believes the file is still over-cap and unaddressed.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC5's verify with `! grep -q 'reducer/_processors\.py' docs/architecture.md` and update file_impact to say 'delete the reducer/_processors.py offenders-table row from docs/architecture.md' — matching the parent's 'graduate off its architecture.md remedy row' mandate.

findings.finding: The AC verification command `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` will fail on the unmodified file: the string `_processors_keys` appears in the body of the existing `reducer/_processors.py` remedy row at docs/architecture.md line 277 ('extract the identity-keyring processor cluster ... into a sibling `_processors_keys.py`'). The plan must also update or replace that prose, not merely delete a non-existent standalone table row.
findings.location: AC: 'The stale offenders-table row is deleted from docs/architecture.md. Verified by: `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's work description, add: 'Update the docs/architecture.md offenders-table remedy row for reducer/_processors.py to replace the old `_processors_keys.py` / 4-function seam description with the new `_processors_identity.py` / 6-function seam description.'

findings.finding: The plan's back-edge-cycle justification for moving _fold_author_attribution is factually wrong: _fold_author_attribution is called by process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520) — all of which stay in _processors.py — not only by process_signature. Moving _fold_author_attribution to _processors_identity.py forces _processors.py to import it back from the leaf, which (a) breaks the 'true leaf / stdlib-free' claim, and (b) means the plan's 'else a back-edge cycle forms' rationale is inverted.
findings.location: Plan body: '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms'
findings.suggested_fix: Keep `_fold_author_attribution` in `_processors.py` and have `_processors_identity.py` import it from there (making the leaf not fully self-contained but avoiding cycles), OR expand the plan to acknowledge the import and retract the 'true leaf' claim for this function.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
